detailed_analysis: src/agents_sync/tool_specs/cursor.py is a Layer-3 (port/wiring) AgenticToolSpec factory for the Cursor agentic_tool. It is a thin, almost entirely declarative module: a single public factory build_cursor_spec(config) that returns a frozen AgenticToolSpec wiring five CustomizationTypeIO cells to functions imported (lazily, inside the factory) from cursor_io and slash_command_io. The code is correct on the happy path, contains no hardcoded secrets, no injection vectors, no mutable default arguments, no silent error swallowing, and no global mutable state. The secret_policy is correctly resolved at call time inside the parse/render closures (lines 36-68, invoked at 57 and 67), so it is re-evaluated at every egress boundary as NFR-15 demands. The two substantive concerns are: (1) a DRY/SRP divergence from its sibling factories — cursor.py hand-rolls a secret_policy() closure plus bespoke parse_mcp_server / render_mcp_server wrappers that duplicate the shared agents_sync.tool_specs._mcp_server_factory.build_mcp_server_io helper that gemini_cli.py and the other MCP tools use; and (2) the public factory body is ~107 lines, over the project's 40-line function limit (though the bulk is a flat declarative dict literal). The more important issue is a SPEC deviation, not a code smell: the module declares io/config cells for the 'agent' and 'skill' customization_types, which contradicts US-10 AC-3's explicit statement that cursor supports {rules, slash_command, mcp_server} only.

internal_imports: agents_sync.cursor_io (parse/render/extract_pair_id for agent, skill, rules, command, mcp_server) — lazy import inside factory
dependency_graph_summary.dependency_rule_compliance: Conforms: a Layer-3 wiring module importing only the port (agentic_tool_spec) and Layer-3 adapters (cursor_io, slash_command_io). No use-case/entity (inward) imports it; no Layer-4 import.

findings.variant: Duplicated logic — bespoke wrappers parallel a shared factory
findings.problem: The secret_policy() resolution closure (lines 36-43) is a byte-for-byte duplicate of the one in src/agents_sync/tool_specs/_mcp_server_factory.py:32-44, and the parse_mcp_server / render_mcp_server wrappers (45-68) re-implement what build_mcp_server_io already provides generically. Sibling factory gemini_cli.py reuses build_mcp_server_io; cursor.py does not. Cursor's deviation is partly justified — cursor_io.parse_cursor_mcp_server_json applies a cursor-specific transport default (_slot_text_with_cursor_transport_default) that the generic factory does not expose — but the secret_policy resolution itself has no such excuse and should not be re-derived per factory. A future change to the policy-key fallback (e.g. retiring the deprecated mcp_server_secret_policy key) must now be edited in every factory that copied it (latent Shotgun Surgery). Maintainability concern only; behaviour is correct.

findings.evidence: def build_cursor_spec(config: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> AgenticToolSpec:  # body spans lines 16-122
findings.problem: build_cursor_spec spans ~107 non-blank lines, exceeding the project's explicit <=40-line function guideline (AGENTS.md §3). Cyclomatic complexity is trivial (2, one if in secret_policy) and the bulk is a flat declarative AgenticToolSpec/io dict literal, so cognitive load is low and this is a soft violation. The nested closures (secret_policy, parse_mcp_server, render_mcp_server, lines 36-68) account for the only real logic and are the part that would justify extraction (see CQ-01). Sibling factories that delegate MCP wiring to build_mcp_server_io are materially shorter.

Storage-mode strings ("single_file", "directory_skill"), file suffixes (".md", ".mdc"), and the JSON map key ("mcpServers") are inline literals rather than named constants/enums. These same magic strings recur verbatim across every tool_specs factory (claude, codex, gemini_cli, copilot, opencode), so they are an implicit shared vocabulary with no single definition. Low severity — they are descriptor data, not logic — but they fit the project's no-inline-magic-strings guideline (AGENTS.md §9) and would be safer as module-level constants or a StorageMode enum on agentic_tool_spec.

deviations.description: US-10 AC-3 states the supported-customization-type example explicitly: 'cursor supports {rules, slash_command, mcp_server} only ... rules over {claude, codex, gemini_cli, cursor}'. The scoped factory instead declares io and config_dir_keys cells for FIVE types: agent (lines 73,80), skill (74,87), rules, slash_command, and mcp_server. Because AgenticToolSpec.supported_customization_types reads io.keys(), this declares cursor as participating in 'agent' and 'skill' customization_artifacts (FR-05 agent matrix, FR-06 skill matrix), directly contradicting AC-3's 'only' clause and its worked example (skill flows over {claude, codex, antigravity}; agent over {claude, codex, gemini_cli} — cursor is absent from both). This is either a code defect (two extra cells that should be removed) or a stale acceptance criterion that the governance docs failed to update when Cursor's surface expanded. Either way the code and the spec disagree; flagging per the audit's deviation mandate. Verify against docs/agentic_tool_integration_protocol.md and project_description glossary before treating as a code bug.

conforming_points: Goal 5 / NFR-11 / US-10 AC-1, AC-2: cursor is a single tool_specs/<name>.py module exposing build_cursor_spec(config) -> AgenticToolSpec; no sync-engine code is referenced or edited. Conforms.

Goal 5 'sync engine never references a concrete tool name': this module IS the adapter/wiring layer where the literal 'cursor' is permitted (architecture §1, §4); no inward use-case module is touched. Conforms.

US-10 AC-6: config_dir_keys (lines 72-78) and io (79-120) declare identical type sets {agent, skill, rules, slash_command, mcp_server}, so the AgenticToolSpec.__post_init__ drift check passes. Conforms.

NFR-15 / Goal 6: secret_policy is resolved at call time inside the parse/render closures (invoked at lines 57 and 67), so it is evaluated at every egress boundary rather than captured once; the cursor MCP parse/render forward secret_policy and agentic_tool_name='cursor' into the policy engine (cursor_io.py:347-378), enabling the NFR-13 structured error to name the tool. Conforms.

Architecture §3 Dependency Rule: imports only the port (agentic_tool_spec) and Layer-3 adapters (cursor_io, slash_command_io), via lazy in-factory imports; no Layer-4 / use-case / entity inward import. Conforms.
No hardcoded secrets (C1), no injection (C2), no silent error swallowing (C3), no mutable default args (C4 — config default is None), no no-op implementations (C5).

verdict_summary: Code is clean and secure (code_score 0.75, zero CRITICAL/MAJOR); the only material issue is a SPEC deviation — cursor declares agent+skill cells, contradicting US-10 AC-3's 'cursor supports {rules, slash_command, mcp_server} only'.
